coredata.dc:title: Photoactive amphiphilic nanoreactor: A chloroplast-like catalyst for natural oxidation of alcohols
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Zhiqiang
Qu, Xuejian
Dai, Jinyu
Zou, Houbing
Zhang, Zongtao
Wang, Runwei
Qiu, Shilun
coredata.dc:description: Exploring catalytic processes performed under natural conditions is interesting, but there remains a great challenge in developing highly efficient catalysts for natural oxidation of alcohols. Herein, we report a chloroplast-like catalyst comprised of photoactive carbon dots (CDs), catalytically active Pt nanoparticles, and amphiphilic nanotubes. Under simulated and real natural reaction conditions, our catalysts exhibited remarkable activity and long-term reusability for the oxidation of various alcohols, significantly outperforming that of other counterpart catalysts and reported thermal/photocatalytic systems. It was demonstrated that when the carbon dots and the amphiphilic nanotubes respectively played a role in the light-harvesting and the substrate transport the Pt/CDs heterointerface acted as the active center for the matter conversion. Such an elaborate cooperation, an advanced process in the photosynthesis of plant, contributed to the excellent catalytic performance. This contribution provides a new design concept for artificial photocatalysts, which is very promising for developing sustainable catalytic processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial chloroplast
Natural photocatalysis
Nanoreactor
Carbon dots
Alcohols oxidation

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the Chloroplast-Like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO
Catalytic performance of Pt/CDs@PMO for natural oxidation of alcohols
The roles of PMO amphiphilicity and photoactive CDs
Implanting CDs into the amphiphilic PMO nanotube (CDs@PMO)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the preparation of the photoactive amphiphilic nanoreactor Pt/CDs@PMO. SEM image (b), TEM images (c, d), HRTEM images (e, f), particle size distribution of the Pt NPs (g), HAADF-STEM images (h, i) and elemental mapping images (j) of the photoactive amphiphilic nanoreactor Pt/CDs@PMO.

(a) Kinetic plots of oxidation of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol over different catalysts. (b) Catalytic efficiency of different catalysts identified from the conversions of nearly 30%. (c) Kinetic plots of natural oxidation of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol over the Pt/CDs@PMO catalyst under different conditions. (d) Turnover frequencies (TOFs) for the Pt/CDs@PMO catalyst under different conditions. The inset in (c) is a photo of the outdoor experiment.

(a) The kinetic curve of the oxidation of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol over catalysts, Pt/CDs@PMO and Pt/CDs@mSiO2. (b) The corresponding catalytic efficiency identified from the conversions of nearly 30%. (c) Adsorption enthalpy analysis of the amphiphilic PMO nanotube towards 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol in water. (d) Adsorption enthalpy analysis of the hydrophilic mSiO2 nanotube towards 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol in water. (e) Water vapor sorption of the amphiphilic PMO nanotube. (f) Water vapor sorption of the hydrophilic mSiO2 nanotube.

(a) The kinetic curve of the oxidation of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol over the Pt/CDs@PMO catalyst with different loadings of CDs. (b) Corresponding catalytic efficiency identified from conversions of nearly 30%. (c) UV–vis adsorption spectra of CDs@PMO with different CDs loading, Pt@PMO and Pt/CDs@PMO. (d) Fluorescence decay spectra of CDs-3@PMO and Pt/CDs-3@PMO excited at 480 nm.

(a) Kinetic plots of oxidation of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol over the Pt/CDs@PMO catalyst under different conditions (adding no sacrificial agent, 0.5 equivalent mannite, 0.5 equivalent carotene and 0.2 equivalent carotene). (b) The corresponding Turnover frequencies (TOFs) identified from the conversions. (c) EPR spectra of the sample after mixing DMPO or TEMPO solution with Pt/CDs@PMO under visible light irradiation or under dark conditions. (d) Possible reaction mechanism for the natural oxidation of alcohol over the chloroplast-like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO.
Oxidation of 4-Methoxybenzyl Alcohol over Different Catalysts under Natural Conditions. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol (1.0 mmol), H2O (8 mL), catalyst (40 mg, Pt 0.164 mol%), ambient temperature (25 °C), open air, visible light irradiation for 8 h.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The product was identified by-GCMS and the only by-product was the corresponding acid.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Oxidation of Various Alcohols over the Pt/CDs@PMO Catalyst under Natural Conditions. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions (indoor): alcohol (0.5 mmol), H2O (4 mL), catalyst (40 mg, Pt 0.164 mol%), ambient temperature (25 °C), open air, visible light radiation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The product was identified by-GCMS and the only by-product was the corresponding acid.

head.ce:title: Photoactive amphiphilic nanoreactor: A chloroplast-like catalyst for natural oxidation of alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, 92 Wucheng Road, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, 92 Wucheng Road, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring catalytic processes performed under natural conditions is interesting, but there remains a great challenge in developing highly efficient catalysts for natural oxidation of alcohols. Herein, we report a chloroplast-like catalyst comprised of photoactive carbon dots (CDs), catalytically active Pt nanoparticles, and amphiphilic nanotubes. Under simulated and real natural reaction conditions, our catalysts exhibited remarkable activity and long-term reusability for the oxidation of various alcohols, significantly outperforming that of other counterpart catalysts and reported thermal/photocatalytic systems. It was demonstrated that when the carbon dots and the amphiphilic nanotubes respectively played a role in the light-harvesting and the substrate transport the Pt/CDs heterointerface acted as the active center for the matter conversion. Such an elaborate cooperation, an advanced process in the photosynthesis of plant, contributed to the excellent catalytic performance. This contribution provides a new design concept for artificial photocatalysts, which is very promising for developing sustainable catalytic processes.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxidation of alcohols to their corresponding carbonyl compounds is widely viewed as one of the most important transformations in both laboratory synthesis and the chemical industry [1,2]. Traditional methods for these reactions often involve the usage of polluting and hazardous chemical oxidants, which increase the amount of plastic waste. From the perspective of environmental friendliness and atomic economy, it is very interesting to use molecular oxygen as a green oxidant to initiate the oxidation reaction [3–5]. Over the past decades, great efforts have been devoted to developing reusable heterogeneous solid catalysts for aerobic oxidation of alcohols in aqueous media [6–8]. For example, various oxide-supported metal (Au, Pd, Pt) catalysts have been reported to show superior catalytic performance for transforming different alcohols to aldehydes/ketones or carboxylic acids [9–12]. Different groups have also explored a series of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs)-derived solid catalysts, where excellent aldehyde/ketone selectivity can be obtained [13,14]. Although these thermal catalytic systems have progressed, they still suffer from low activities and harsh reaction conditions such as high temperature, high pressure, and the addition of corrosive additives (KOH or K2CO3).

Visible light-driven photocatalysis is a promising process to realize highly efficient alcohol oxidation because it allows for solar energy to activate inert molecular oxygen under mild conditions [15–22]. Metal nanoparticle/semiconductor heterojunctions are typical photocatalysts for alcohol oxidation [23–29]. Owing to the efficient charge separation at the interface, active oxygen species can be easily produced by photogenerated hot electrons, and where high photocatalytic activity was observed at nearly natural reaction conditions. For instance, different plasmonic metal nanoparticles have been loaded on titanium oxide to facilitate electron transfer and enhanced activities [30–33]. Recently, Jiang’s group employed semiconductor-like porphyritic MOFs as new photosensitizers to manipulate the formation of single oxygen and achieve the highly selective oxidation of aromatic alcohols to aldehydes [34]. Other photosensitizers, such as dye molecules [35–37], C3N4 [38–40], and porous polymers [41,42] have been used to promote photocatalytic activities. These studies have shown that it is a facile and efficient strategy for improving photocatalytic efficiency to modulate the Schottky barrier of heterostructures via physicochemical property adjustment of metal nanoparticles and semiconductors. However, most of the focus has been on the optimization of the transfer of hot electrons at the heterointerface, and almost no one focuses on the substrate transport inside photocatalysts, which is also an important factor for catalytic activity.

Chloroplast, a natural photocatalyst containing an outer envelope and chlorophyll as well as stroma, can convert CO2 and water into carbohydrates by utilizing solar energy as the driving force. When the chlorophyll acts as a light-harvester, the envelope and stroma (comprising bioactive enzymes) play crucial roles in the transport and transformation of organic molecules in plants. It is widely known that the elaborate cooperation of these components endows the chloroplast with high photosynthetic efficiency. Inspired by this work, recently, artificial photosynthesis has received increasing attention and several methods have been developed to construct plant-like photocatalysts for CO2 capture and transformation [43–45]. There continues to be a demand exploring new routes towards artificial photocatalysts for different reactions before practical applications.

Herein, we have designed and prepared a chloroplast-like photocatalyst by implanting carbon dots (CDs) and platinum nanoparticles (Pt NPs) into amphiphilic periodic mesoporous organosilica (PMO) nanotube for the natural oxidation of alcohols. Under both indoor and outdoor reaction conditions, our catalyst exhibits remarkable activity and stability for the aerobic oxidation of alcohols, which is much higher than that of reported thermal catalytic systems and metal/semiconductor heterostructures. The role of every component was carefully studied in the sustainable oxidation process. The superior amphiphilicity of the PMO nanotube was found to strengthen both the adsorption of hydrophobic alcohols from water and desorption of product water molecules from the active sites. The photoactive CDs were responsible for absorbing sunlight, and the Pt/CDs heterointerface served as the active center. The hot electrons of CDs smoothly flowed to Pt NPs upon photo-irradiation and regulated the electronic density of the Pt surface, which was beneficial to the formation of active oxygen species and improved the catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the Chloroplast-Like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a illustrates the preparation of the photoactive amphiphilic nanoreactor Pt/CDs@PMO. Amphiphilic PMO nanotubes with a hybrid framework were obtained according to our previous publications [46,47] (Figure S1). Then the CDs, emerging light-absorbing nanomaterials with excellent optical properties and photostability [48,49], were selectively implanted into the nanotubes cavity via a facile strategy of “ship in bottle”, obtaining the CDs@PMO sample (Figure S2). Finally, the catalytically active Pt NPs were introduced into the sample CDs@PMO by a photo-reduction method, leading to the formation of the chloroplast-like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO. Since the photoexcited CDs acted as electron donors to reduce the Pt salt on the particle surfaces, the formed Pt NPs were precisely loaded on the CDs surfaces [50]. The SEM and TEM images clearly show that the sample Pt/CDs@PMO has a morphology of one dimensional nanotube with an identical particle size (200 nm in width; 600–800 nm in length) and wall thickness (25 nm) with those of the PMO nanotubes (Fig. 1b, c). This suggests that the process of loading CDs and Pt NPs has no influence on the structures of PMO nanotubes, owing to their superior hydrothermal stability. The high magnification TEM image reveals that almost all of the CDs and Pt NPs were confined inside the cavity of the PMO nanotubes (Fig. 1d). Any CDs and Pt NPs cannot be observed outside of the nanotubes. The radial mesochannels are also visible in the wall of nanotubes. Interestingly, every CDs particle is surrounded with many tiny Pt NPs, highlighted by the yellow arrows in Fig. 1d. The HRTEM image and particle size distribution further confirm that the uniform Pt NPs with an average particle diameter of 2 nm are loaded on the surfaces of the CDs particles (Fig. 1e, g).

This suggests that there would be a strong interaction between the Pt NPs and the CDs particle, which might present a unique heterointerface for improving the efficiency of charge separation. A distinct lattice fringe with lattice spacings of 0.22 nm, assigned to the (111) plane of face-centered cube (fcc) Pt, is also visible in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1f). The PXRD shows a series of diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 39.9°, 46.4°, and 67.7° (Figure S3a), indicating the presence of crystalline Pt NPs in the sample. The HAADF-STEM images further prove that both CDs and Pt NPs are loaded inside the nanotube (Fig. 1h, i) and the Pt NPs encircle the CDs particle (yellow cycles in Fig. 1i). As evidenced by the EDX elemental mapping in Fig. 1j, all of the elements, including Si, C, O, N and Pt, are homogeneously distributed throughout the nanotube. The elemental analysis and inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis further reveal that the loading amount of CDs and Pt NPs is 5.5 wt% and 0.78 wt% (Table S1), respectively. According to the nitrogen sorption analysis (Figure S3b), the sample Pt/CDs@PMO shows a BET surface area of 556 m2 g−1 and a pore volume of 1.04 cm3 g−1, which is lower than those of the sample PMO nanotube and the sample CDs@PMO (Table S2). All these results clearly demonstrate that we have successfully obtained a catalyst containing three different components that act as light harvesters (CDs), substrate transporters (PMO tube wall) and matter converters (Pt NPs), respectively. Such a distinct structure is similar to that of a chloroplast, a natural catalyst for photosynthesis. For comparison, we also prepared a sample Pt@PMO without CDs (Figure S4) and a sample Pt/CDs@mSiO2 with a hydrophilic SiO2 tube wall (Figure S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance of Pt/CDs@PMO for natural oxidation of alcohols
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic power of Pt/CDs@PMO was evaluated using 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol as the model substrate under a simulated natural condition (H2O as the solvent, atmospheric pressure of air as the oxidant, visible light irradiation, ambient temperature (initially, 25 °C), without any additives). The results summarized in the Table 1 indicate that the Pt/CDs@PMO sample shows the highest catalytic activity amongst all catalysts. With an extremely low Pt dosage (610 of S/C), the substrate was fully transformed into corresponding aldehydes and carboxylic acids with an aldehyde selectivity of 93% over 8 h (entry 1). Such an activity is significantly higher than those obtained without light irradiation (14.4%) and even with an application of external heat (80 °C, 26.2%). This suggests that visible light irradiation plays a crucial role in enhancing the activity of Pt/CDs@PMO. Using the sample Pt/@PMO (entry 2) and CDs@PMO (entry 3) as the catalyst, conversions of only 62.4% and 42.5% were obtained under the same reaction conditions, respectively. The sample Pt/CDs@mSiO2 with a hydrophilic tube wall also gave a much lower activity with a conversion of 73.7% (entry 5). These results reveal that three distinct components of Pt/CDs@PMO are essential for its excellent catalytic activity. Furthermore, the activity enhancement achieved by visible light irradiation for the sample Pt/CDs@PMO was found to be significantly higher than other samples (Figure S6). This suggests that the Pt/CDs interface promotes the oxidation reaction, which can be further confirmed by the much lower activity (74.0%) of the physical mixture of Pt/@PMO and CDs@PMO (entry 4). It is worth mentioning that the activity of the commercial Pt/C catalyst was still much lower than that of Pt/CDs@PMO under visible light irradiation in spite of higher activity under dark conditions (entry 7), further highlighting the importance of the Pt/CDs interface.

The oxidation kinetics were then investigated to further examine the advantages of the catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO. As shown in the Fig. 2 a, Pt/CDs@PMO exhibit the highest reaction rate among all catalysts, and the visible light irradiation significantly facilitated the oxidation rate of Pt/CDs@PMO. Interestingly, all catalysts showed a nearly linear relationship between conversion and reaction time under visible light irradiation, suggesting typical zero-order kinetics. On the contrary, under dark conditions the catalyst presented pseudo first-order kinetics, as evidenced by the linear relationship between In (Ct/C0) and reaction time (Figure S7). This suggests that visible light irradiation may alter the reaction mechanism of Pt/CDs@PMO (to be discussed later). According to the reaction kinetics, catalytic efficiency of Pt/CDs@PMO was calculated to be as high as 79.6 h−1 (Fig. 2b), which was 7.2 times and 4.0 times higher than that obtained without light irradiation and with external heat (80 °C), respectively. Such an activity level was also 1.8–2.6 times higher than that of the sample without CDs or Pt NPs. Based-on the Pt dispersity (24.9%) measured by CO chemisorption, the TOF was calculated to be as high as 306 h−1. Considering the excellent activity realized under current extremely sustainable conditions, we performed an outdoor catalytic experiment (25–30 °C) with an identical catalyst dosage (Fig. 2c). It was found that the reaction also followed zero-order kinetics and the activity was slightly lower than that obtained under indoor conditions due to the limited light intensity. The calculated TOF still calculated reached as high as 290.7 h−1 (Fig. 2d), which was still higher than that of most of the reported catalysts (Table S3). Notably, our Pt/CDs@PMO is one of the most active catalysts for sustainable oxidation of alcohols. Combining all of the above experimental results, we can conclude that the cooperation of the three distinct components of Pt/CDs@PMO is responsible for the remarkable activity in the natural oxidation of alcohols, and the Pt/CDs interface plays an important role in promoting the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The roles of PMO amphiphilicity and photoactive CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the role of every component, we studied the effect of both PMO amphiphilicity and photoactive CDs on the oxidation activity. As shown in Fig. 3 a, both amphiphilic Pt/CDs@PMO and hydrophilic Pt/CDs@mSiO2 show zero-order kinetics and the reaction rate of the former catalyst is higher than that of the latter catalyst. A 1.5-fold enhanced activity is observed (Fig. 3b). Considering their identical active component, such an enhancement should be ascribed to the amphiphilicity of PMO nanotube (Figure S8). Then we carefully examined the reactant adsorption and the product desorption on two different supports. The PMO nanotube absorbed 807.5 mg/g of 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol from aqueous solution at an equilibration concentration under ambient conditions (25 °C), which was much higher than that of mSiO2 nanotube (475.6 mg/g, Figure S9). Based on the Clausius-Clapeyron equation [51,52], the adsorption enthalpy (ΔrHΘm) can be identified by fitting the equilibration data (Fig. 3c, d). It can be clearly seen that the ΔrHΘm of the PMO nanotube was − 8.67 kJ/mol, which is close to our previous results [52] and is two times higher than that of the mSiO2 nanotube (−4.25 kJ/mol). This result strongly proves that the PMO nanotube has a higher affinity to hydrophobic alcohols in aqueous solution. Further-more, we tested the adsorption–desorption kinetics of two supports towards water molecules that are one of products in the aerobic oxidation of alcohols (Fig. 3e, f). As expected, the PMO nanotube displayed not only a much lower adsorption capacity but also slower adsorption kinetics than the mSiO2 nanotube. More interestingly, the PMO nanotube realized largely faster desorption kinetics. The adsorbed water molecules were almost completely desorbed from the PMO nanotube at a humidity level of 32%, while the mSiO2 nanotube still showed an adsorption capacity of 3 wt% after the desorption process. This is likely due to the strong hydrogen bond interactions between the water molecules and polar surface (silanol groups). These results clearly demonstrate that the superior amphiphilicity of Pt/CDs@PMO strengthens both reactant adsorption from water and desorption of byproduct water molecules from active sites, thus leading to enhanced oxidation activity.

Next, the CDs loading of Pt/CDs@PMO was adjusted to clarify their contributions on the photocatalytic activity by altering the implantation cycles. After implanting the CDs three times, elemental analysis showed that the C and N content and the N/C ratio were increased (Table S1), suggesting a rise in the CDs weight ratio. According to the reaction kinetics in Fig. 4 a, the reaction rate of alcohol oxidation was gradually enhanced along with the increase in CDs loading. The corresponding catalytic efficiency improved from 57.9 to 79.6 h−1 (Fig. 4b), presenting a 1.37-fold activity enhancement. Since the CDs loading on Pt/CDs-3@PMO is still exceptionally low (≤6 wt%, Table S1), such an activity enhancement is significant. The UV–vis spectra in Fig. 4c shows that there are several peaks appearing on three CDs@PMO samples, which cannot be seen on the Pt/@PMO sample. Notably, these peaks stem from the light adsorption of CDs and should be assigned to the n − π* and/or π − π* transitions. It can be further observed that the adsorption of Pt/CDs-3@PMO under the visible light region (≥450 nm) is much stronger than that of CDs-3@PMO and Pt/@PMO, which likely results from the unique interaction between the Pt NPs and the CDs particles. Moreover, with the increase in CDs loading, the CDs@PMO samples display enhanced light adsorption. These phenomena confirm that the CDs serve as a light-harvester, and the improved light adsorption produces a higher oxidation activity.

The photoluminescence and fluorescence lifetime were tested to investigate the electron transfer property at the Pt/CDs interface. The CDs prepared under the same conditions for CDs@PMO exhibited a maximum emission centered at 550 nm upon excitation with a wavelength from 440 to 520 nm, and an excitation-independent behavior was presented in the emission spectrum (Figure S10). This optical property is similar to results in previous literature. However, the samples of CDs@PMO and Pt/CDs@PMO showed significantly decreased luminescence intensity with respect to the free CDs (Figure S11). Particularly, a nearly quenched emission was observed after loading Pt NPs. Moreover, the time-resolved transient fluorescence decay spectra indicate that Pt/CDs@PMO had a distinctly shorter emission lifetime than CDs@PMO (Fig. 4d), suggesting a faster decay of excitons of excited CDs in the presence of Pt NPs. These observations reveal that rapid interfacial electron transfer occurred from excited CDs to Pt NPs [53], which may facilitate the charge separation at the Pt/CDs interface and improve the utilization of the photogenerated electrons/holes.

To identify the reactive oxygen species for the natural oxidation of alcohols with the catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO, mannite and carotene were respectively used as scavengers for
OH and 1O2 to be introduced into the reaction system [34]. As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, mannite did not show any impact on the both alcohol conversion and aldehyde selectivity, excluding
OH as the reactive oxygen specie. In sharp contrast, 0.2 equivalent of carotene could inhibit the oxidation reaction obviously and 0.5 equivalent of caro-tene significantly decreased the TOFs from 306 h−1 to 75.5 h−1. These results suggested that the possible reactive oxygen specie involved in our reaction system is 1O2.

Then electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spin-trapping was used to confirm which reactive oxygen species were produced at the Pt/CDs interface. When using dimethylpyridine N-oxide (DMPO) as the radical trapping agent, as shown in Fig. 5c, the EPR spectrum did not show any signals in the presence of Pt/CDs@PMO under both light-radiation and dark conditions. This indicated that both O2
- and
OH should not be viewed as the responsible oxidants in the current catalytic process. Moreover, employing another spin-trapping reagent, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO), with a superior ability to trap 1O2, a characteristic 1:1:1 triplet signal of nitroxide radical 4-oxo-TEMPO was present in the EPR spectrum under light-radiation. On the contrary, the signal was barely detected under dark conditions. These results reveal that 1O2 was the reactive species promoting the photocatalytic oxidation reaction.

Based on the above results and literature knowledge [53,54], a possible mechanism was proposed for the natural alcohol oxidation over the chloroplast-like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, because of the superior affinity of amphiphilic PMO toward organics in water, the alcohol molecules were enriched around the Pt/CDs heterointerface inside the PMO nanotubes. The high reactant concentration was beneficial to increasing the reaction rate. Subsequently, after the CDs were photoexcited by sunlight, the excited electrons smoothly flowed to the Pt NPs located on the surfaces of CDs. Such an interfacial charge transfer not only improved photocarrier generation and charge separation but also enabled the accumulation of hot electrons on the Pt surfaces. This promoted the activation of O2 to the reactive 1O2 species that were reported to possess superior oxidation power [54], thus enhancing the photo-oxidation activity. Moreover, it should be pointed out that such a special reactive oxygen species was not presented under dark conditions and even at 80 °C. Different reactive oxygen species may give a different reaction mechanism, in which light irradiation induced the alteration of reaction kinetics from pseudo first-order to zero-order. Finally, the product water molecules fast desorbed from the Pt/CDs interface and diffused out of the catalyst. This immediate removal of the product from the reaction system was favorable to shifting the reaction equilibrium and pushing the reaction forward.

Finally, we examined the substrate scope and reusability of the chloroplast-like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO. As displayed in Table 2 , benzylic alcohols substituted with various electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups could be easily transformed with a good conversion (66%-90%) under current natural conditions (entries 1–7). Among these primary aromatic alcohols, the 4-fluorobenzyl alcohol gave a low conversion of only 31%, which was likely due to its super-hydrophobicity (entry 5). A superior activity was also obtained in the oxidation of less active allylic alcohols, such as cinnamyl alcohol (entry 8). In addition, secondary aromatic alcohols can be effectively converted to their corresponding ketones at a conversion of > 75% with a selectivity of > 99% (entries 9–10). These results demonstrate the wide substrate tolerance of our catalyst for the natural oxidation of alcohols.

Furthermore, we found that Pt/CDs@PMO possessed excellent catalytic stability (Figure S12). After reuse over ten reactions, a conversion of nearly 100% could be still obtained under the same conditions, despite the slightly varied selectivity. The liquid phase of the reaction mixture was collected after the tenth run and the ICP analysis revealed that only ppb Pt species could be detected. The reused catalyst was then carefully characterized. The TEM images indicate that there are no distinct changes in the recovered Pt/CDs@PMO (Figure S13). Similar to the fresh catalyst, the tiny Pt NPs with particle sizes of 2.2 nm were still loaded on the surfaces of CDs. The XPS and UV–visible spectra reveal that the electronic state of Pt NPs and the light-harvesting ability of CDs remain consistent with those of the fresh catalyst (Figure S13). These results verify the superior confinement effect of PMO nanotubes and the good photostability of CDs contributing to the excellent catalytic stability of Pt/CDs@PMO.

In summary, we have reported a new design concept for artificial photocatalysts by integrating photoactive and catalytically active materials into an amphiphilic hollow nanostructure. The used CDs can effectively absorb sun-light, and the employed PMO nanotubes can facilitate the transport of reactants and products due to their superior hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity. Moreover, the created CDs/Pt heterointerfaces inside the particles promote photo-carrier generation and charge separation by rapid interfacial electron transfer, producing reactive oxygen species efficiently for matter conversion. Based on the elaborate cooperation of these components, the obtained chloroplast-like catalyst exhibited remarkable catalytic activity and stability for the natural oxidation of alcohols, significantly outperforming that of reported thermal catalytic systems and metal/semiconductor heterostructures. We believe that the presented strategy in this work will create great opportunities for designing innovatory artificial photocatalysts to develop new sustainable technology.

Ethylene glycol, aqueous ammonia (NH3·H2O, 28%), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 98%), chloroform, diethylamine, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%), ethyl acetate, and sodium tetrachloropalladate (H2PtCl6, 98%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. 1,2-Bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTEE, 98%) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. All alcohols were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implanting CDs into the amphiphilic PMO nanotube (CDs@PMO)
ce:section.ce:para: A strategy of “ship in bottle” was employed to implant the CDs into the amphiphilic PMO nanotubes prepared according to our previous publications [47]. Typically, the PMO nanotubes (0.5 g) were dispersed in a mixture of chloroform (10.2 mL) and diethylamine (3.4 mL), and the gel-like solution was refluxed at 90 °C for 12 h. The solution gradually turned from colorless to orange. The precipitates were then collected by centrifugation and washed with ethyl alcohol five times until the supernatant had no fluorescence effect, which provided the CDs@PMO sample. By repeating the process, we could obtain CDs@PMO with different loadings of CDs.

Pt nanoparticles were introduced into the sample CDs@PMO by a photo-reduction method for preparing the chloroplast-like catalyst Pt/CDs@PMO. Typically, 20 mM H2PtCl6 (1.0 mL) was added dropwise into the solution containing 40 mL of H2O and 200 mg of CDs@PMO. Then the mixed solution was stirred for 12 h at room temperature under visible light radiation. The precipitates were then collected by centrifugation and washed three times with water and ethanol respectively. After drying at 80 °C under vacuum for 6 h, we obtained the Pt/CDs@PMO catalyst. The loading of Pt was determined to be 0.78 wt%.

In the study of catalytic oxidation, 1 mmol of alcohol, 40 mg of supported Pt catalysts (The molar ratio of Pt and substrate is 0.164%), and 8.0 mL of H2O were mixed in a 50 mL quartzose round-bottom flask equipped with a reflux condenser and a magnetic stirrer. The reaction irradiated under a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter (PLS-SEX300C, Beijing Perfectlight Technology) or solar (from 8:30 am to 16:30 pm, the temperature is between 25 °C and 30 °C) and magnetic stirring (stirring rate: 500 rpm) for 8 h (10 h,16 h or 20 h). After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was extracted by ethyl acetate three times. The liquid phase was subsequently analyzed using a gas chromatography-mass spectrum Shimadzu GCMS-QP2010 Plus, using n-decane as an internal standard. The column type is GsBP-1 (30 m), the initial temperature was 120 °C, the heating rate was 10 °C/min, and the final temperature was 250 °C.

The conversion and the selectivity are defined as conv. (%) = [(C0 − CS)/C0] × 100% and sel. (%) = [CP/(C0 − CS)] × 100%, where C0 is the initial concentration of substrate, CS and CP are the concentrations of the detected substrate and product, respectively. Catalytic efficiency (h−1) is measured as moles of products total Pt atoms molar per hour at the reaction time. The sample CDs@PMO was calculated according the sample Pt/CDs@PMO. Turnover frequency (TOF, h−1) is measured as molar of products per surface Pt atoms (total Pt atoms molar * metal dispersion) per hour at the reaction time.
For the recycling test, the catalyst was collected by centrifugation and washed two times with ethanol and water respectively, and then used directly for the next catalytic reaction.

The morphology and structure of prepared samples was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JSM-6700F, JEOL, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 S-Twin F20, FEI, Netherlands). Powder XRD patterns were obtained using a Rigaku 2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were gotten from Sorptometer (ASAP 2020 Micromeritics USA). Samples were degassed for 10 h at 120 °C. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas were calculated from 0.05 to 0.30 of the P/P0. Pore size distribution was estimated from the adsorption branch of the isotherm by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The total pore volume was calculated from the adsorbed amount at P/P0 = 0.995. The UV–visible spectra were got from UV/VIS spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2450). The content of C, H, N elemental were get from elemental analyser (Elementer, vario micro, Germany) Fluorescence spectra were obtained from fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, FLS 920 UK) The EPR spectra was get from Electron spin resonance spectrometer (JEOL, JES-FA200) The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained from photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250 USA). CO pulse adsorption measurements were tested using Micromeritics AutochemⅡ2920. First, the sample was heated to 400 ℃ for an hour at H2/Ar (10%) atmosphere, then return to room temperature. And CO pulses were injected at this temperature until the adsorption reached saturation. The metal dispersion was calculated by assuming a CO to surface metal atom ratio of 1:1. The metal dispersion was 24.9%.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21703128, U1510105, 21733009, 21771082 and 21573136). Also, we are grateful for the test platform provided by the Shanxi University of Scientific Instrument Center.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127243.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
